content.text: Idempotent Writes & Upserts in Data Pipelines

Data pipelines rarely run perfectly the first time. Networks drop, servers restart, jobs get retried, and the same data gets processed more than once. Without careful design, this leads to duplicate rows, inflated counts, and corrupted datasets.
are the two core techniques that make pipelines safe to retry without producing garbage.

means that running an operation once produces exactly the same result as running it ten times. An idempotent write is a database or file operation where re-sending the same data never changes the final state beyond what the first write already established.

) is a single operation that checks whether a record already exists: if it does, update it; if it does not, insert it. Upserts are the most common mechanism used to achieve idempotent writes in practice.
"What happens if my pipeline runs twice?"
"Exactly the same result as running it once."
In any real pipeline, failures and retries are guaranteed. Consider these common scenarios:
A network timeout causes a write to fail, but the data was actually committed. The retry writes it again, creating a duplicate.
A daily batch job is accidentally triggered twice. Every record appears twice in the data warehouse.
A streaming consumer crashes mid-batch. On restart, it replays messages from the last checkpoint, re-processing records it already wrote.
A pipeline is backfilled for a historical date range that already has partial data.
Without idempotency, all of these scenarios silently corrupt the dataset. Duplicates and partial overwrites are among the most common causes of data quality incidents in production pipelines.
Pipelines built on idempotent writes gain three foundational properties:
Any step can be retried without human intervention. This is the basis of automatic failure recovery and is what allows orchestrators like Airflow and Prefect to retry failed tasks confidently.
Running the pipeline again for any date range, any partition, or any batch produces a deterministic, consistent result — not an accumulated mess of duplicates.
When writes are idempotent, error recovery logic collapses to "retry the whole thing." There is no need for complex compensating transactions or manual deduplication.
Nothing is free, and idempotency introduces real costs:
Checking for existing records before writing (as upserts do) is always slower than a pure
. On large datasets this can be significant.
Upserts require the database to check and potentially lock rows before writing. Under high concurrency, this creates lock contention and reduced throughput.
Idempotency often requires a unique key or constraint to be defined upfront. Retrofitting this onto an existing table with dirty data is painful.
Some storage systems (plain CSV files, many columnar stores, some NoSQL databases) have no built-in upsert. Idempotency must be implemented at the application layer.
To achieve idempotency without upserts (e.g., delete-and-reinsert), pipelines need to track which partitions or batches they own, adding orchestration complexity.
There are four distinct strategies for achieving idempotent writes. Each suits different storage systems and pipeline patterns.

The database is asked to insert a row. If a row with the same primary key (or unique constraint) already exists, it updates the specified columns instead. The uniqueness constraint is the contract that prevents duplicates.

-- PostgreSQL syntax
INSERT INTO events (event_id, user_id, event_type, created_at, payload)
VALUES (%s, %s, %s, %s, %s)
ON CONFLICT (event_id)
DO UPDATE SET
    payload = EXCLUDED.payload,
    updated_at = NOW();
-- MySQL / MariaDB syntax
INSERT INTO events (event_id, user_id, event_type, payload)
VALUES (%s, %s, %s, %s)
ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE
    payload = VALUES(payload),
    updated_at = NOW();
-- SQLite syntax (identical to PostgreSQL for this case)
INSERT INTO events (event_id, user_id, event_type, payload)
VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?)
ON CONFLICT(event_id) DO UPDATE SET
    payload = excluded.payload;
-- Snowflake / BigQuery / DuckDB: MERGE statement
MERGE INTO events AS target
USING (SELECT %s AS event_id, %s AS payload) AS source
ON target.event_id = source.event_id
WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET target.payload = source.payload
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT (event_id, payload) VALUES (source.event_id, source.payload);

For batch pipelines processing a complete date partition, the cleanest approach is to delete all existing data for that partition and then reinsert the fresh batch. This avoids the row-by-row overhead of upserts entirely.
This is idempotent because re-running deletes the old data and replaces it with an identical set. The final state is always exactly the current batch's data for that partition.
Strategy 3: INSERT IGNORE / ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING
When records are immutable (events, log lines, transactions) and the goal is simply to skip already-written rows:

-- PostgreSQL
INSERT INTO events (event_id, user_id, created_at)
VALUES (%s, %s, %s)
ON CONFLICT (event_id) DO NOTHING;
This inserts new rows and silently discards duplicates. It works when the source of truth is the incoming data and the existing rows should never be overwritten.
For append-only stores (like S3, HDFS, or BigQuery raw tables), write all incoming data freely and apply deduplication as a downstream step:

# In PySpark or pandas, deduplicate after loading
df = df.drop_duplicates(subset=["event_id"], keep="last")

This is not idempotent at the storage layer, but the pipeline result is idempotent because deduplication always produces the same canonical output regardless of how many times the raw data was written.
Step 1 — Define a natural unique key
Every idempotent write strategy depends on being able to identify a row uniquely. This key should come from the source data, not the database's auto-increment. Common patterns:
from the source system (UUID or surrogate key)
A content hash of the record's fields when no ID exists
Step 2 — Add a unique constraint to the table

CREATE TABLE events (
    id          BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    event_id    TEXT NOT NULL,
    user_id     BIGINT NOT NULL,
    event_type  TEXT NOT NULL,
    payload     JSONB,
    created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,
    updated_at  TIMESTAMPTZ,

    CONSTRAINT uq_events_event_id UNIQUE (event_id)
);

from sqlalchemy.dialects.postgresql import insert as pg_insert
from sqlalchemy import create_engine, Table, MetaData

engine = create_engine("postgresql+psycopg2://user:pass@host/db")
metadata = MetaData()
events_table = Table("events", metadata, autoload_with=engine)

def upsert_events(records: list[dict]):
    if not records:
        return

    stmt = pg_insert(events_table).values(records)
    stmt = stmt.on_conflict_do_update(
        index_elements=["event_id"],    # the unique constraint column
        set_={
            "payload":    stmt.excluded.payload,
            "updated_at": stmt.excluded.updated_at,
        }
    )

    with engine.begin() as conn:
        result = conn.execute(stmt)
        print(f"Upserted {result.rowcount} rows")
Step 4 — Implement partition replacement in Python

from sqlalchemy import text

def replace_partition(engine, table: str, partition_col: str,
                       partition_value: str, records: list[dict]):
    """
    Atomically replace all data for a given partition.
    The DELETE and INSERT happen in one transaction.
    """
    if not records:
        return

    with engine.begin() as conn:
        # 1. Delete existing data for this partition
        conn.execute(
            text(f"DELETE FROM {table} WHERE {partition_col} = :val"),
            {"val": partition_value}
        )
        # 2. Insert the fresh batch
        conn.execute(
            text(f"""
                INSERT INTO {table} ({', '.join(records[0].keys())})
                VALUES ({', '.join(':' + k for k in records[0].keys())})
            """),
            records
        )
        print(f"Replaced {len(records)} rows in {table} for {partition_value}")
Step 5 — Implement upsert with raw psycopg2 (high-performance bulk upsert)
from psycopg2 with a single SQL statement is far faster than looping:

import psycopg2
from psycopg2.extras import execute_values

def bulk_upsert(conn, records: list[dict]):
    """
    High-performance bulk upsert using psycopg2 execute_values.
    Sends all rows in a single round-trip.
    """
    sql = """
        INSERT INTO events (event_id, user_id, event_type, payload, created_at)
        VALUES %s
        ON CONFLICT (event_id)
        DO UPDATE SET
            payload    = EXCLUDED.payload,
            updated_at = NOW()
    """
    # Convert list of dicts to list of tuples in column order
    rows = [
        (r["event_id"], r["user_id"], r["event_type"],
         json.dumps(r["payload"]), r["created_at"])
        for r in records
    ]

    with conn.cursor() as cur:
        execute_values(cur, sql, rows, page_size=1000)
    conn.commit()
    print(f"Bulk upserted {len(rows)} rows")
Step 6 — Implement upsert with pandas + SQLAlchemy
When data arrives as a pandas DataFrame:

import pandas as pd
from sqlalchemy.dialects.postgresql import insert as pg_insert

def upsert_dataframe(df: pd.DataFrame, engine, table_name: str,
                      unique_col: str, update_cols: list[str]):
    records = df.to_dict(orient="records")
    metadata = MetaData()
    table = Table(table_name, metadata, autoload_with=engine)

    stmt = pg_insert(table).values(records)
    update_dict = {col: stmt.excluded[col] for col in update_cols}
    stmt = stmt.on_conflict_do_update(
        index_elements=[unique_col],
        set_=update_dict
    )

    with engine.begin() as conn:
        conn.execute(stmt)
Step 7 — Implement upsert for DuckDB (analytics pipelines)
DuckDB is popular for local analytical pipelines and supports

import duckdb

def upsert_duckdb(df: pd.DataFrame, db_path: str, table: str):
    con = duckdb.connect(db_path)

    # Register the DataFrame as a temporary view
    con.register("staging", df)

    con.execute(f"""
        INSERT OR REPLACE INTO {table}
        SELECT * FROM staging
    """)
    con.close()

Consider a pipeline that ingests clickstream events from a Kafka topic into a PostgreSQL analytics database. The Kafka consumer commits offsets only after a successful batch write. If the write fails (network glitch, DB timeout), the consumer restarts from the last committed offset — meaning it will re-process and re-send the same events.
Without idempotency, every retry creates duplicate rows. With bulk upsert keyed on
, the retry simply overwrites rows that were already written with identical data — no duplicates, no corruption.

from kafka import KafkaConsumer
import json, psycopg2
from psycopg2.extras import execute_values

BATCH_SIZE = 500

consumer = KafkaConsumer(
    "clickstream",
    bootstrap_servers="kafka:9092",
    group_id="pg-loader",
    enable_auto_commit=False,       # Manual commit for at-least-once guarantee
    auto_offset_reset="earliest",
    value_deserializer=lambda m: json.loads(m.decode("utf-8"))
)

conn = psycopg2.connect("postgresql://user:pass@db/analytics")

UPSERT_SQL = """
    INSERT INTO clicks (event_id, user_id, page, ts, metadata)
    VALUES %s
    ON CONFLICT (event_id)
    DO UPDATE SET
        metadata = EXCLUDED.metadata,
        updated_at = NOW()
"""

batch = []
for message in consumer:
    batch.append((
        message.value["event_id"],
        message.value["user_id"],
        message.value["page"],
        message.value["ts"],
        json.dumps(message.value.get("metadata", {}))
    ))

    if len(batch) >= BATCH_SIZE:
        with conn.cursor() as cur:
            execute_values(cur, UPSERT_SQL, batch, page_size=500)
        conn.commit()
        consumer.commit()   # Only commit offset after successful DB write
        batch = []
ensures Kafka offsets are only advanced after the database write succeeds.
means replaying the same messages is harmless.
sends the entire batch in one SQL statement, minimizing round-trips.
Using auto-increment IDs as the idempotency key.

Database-generated IDs (SERIAL, IDENTITY, AUTO_INCREMENT) are assigned at insert time. If the same logical record is inserted twice, it gets two different IDs — two rows, both "unique" by the database's definition. The idempotency key must come from the source data.
Not wrapping delete-then-insert in a single transaction.
If the delete succeeds but the insert fails, the partition is now empty. Always perform both operations inside a single
If the incoming data has been corrected or enriched since the original write,
will silently discard the update. This is only correct for truly immutable data like event logs.
Not handling the case where the unique constraint doesn't exist yet.
will throw a syntax error at runtime, not a conflict. Always verify the constraint exists before deploying upsert logic.
per record in a Python loop is catastrophically slow. A table of 100,000 rows becomes 100,000 individual round-trips. Always batch with
clause will overwrite the original creation timestamp on every retry. Fields like
, and original source metadata should generally be excluded from the update clause.
Ignoring partial failures in a batch.

If a batch upsert of 1,000 rows fails halfway through and no transaction wraps it, 500 rows are written and 500 are not. The next retry will successfully upsert all 1,000, but only because the first 500 hit the
path. This works, but only if the entire batch is retried atomically.
Wrap every batch write — whether upsert or delete-then-insert — in a single database transaction. Either the whole batch succeeds or none of it does. This is the foundation of atomicity.
Choose the right strategy for the data's mutability:
Aim for 500–5,000 rows per statement depending on row size and database. Use
clause gives an audit trail that shows when a record was last re-processed, which is invaluable for debugging pipeline reruns.
Every data pipeline test suite should include a test that runs the load function twice with the same input data and asserts the row count equals the expected unique count, not double:

def test_upsert_is_idempotent(engine, sample_records):
    upsert_events(sample_records)
    upsert_events(sample_records)   # run again with same data

    with engine.connect() as conn:
        count = conn.execute(text("SELECT COUNT(*) FROM events")).scalar()

    assert count == len(sample_records)  # NOT 2x the records
clause lets the upsert report back exactly which rows were inserted vs. updated, enabling useful logging:

stmt = pg_insert(events_table).values(records)
stmt = stmt.on_conflict_do_update(
    index_elements=["event_id"],
    set_={"payload": stmt.excluded.payload}
).returning(events_table.c.event_id, literal_column("xmax = 0").label("is_insert"))

with engine.begin() as conn:
    result = conn.execute(stmt).fetchall()
    inserts = sum(1 for r in result if r.is_insert)
    updates = len(result) - inserts
    print(f"{inserts} inserted, {updates} updated")
is 0 for newly inserted rows and non-zero for updated rows.)
Upserts have inherently higher overhead than pure inserts because the database must:
Look up the index for each incoming row to check for conflicts.
Either skip (DO NOTHING) or update the existing row.
This cost becomes significant at scale. Strategies for high-volume upserts:
Rather than upserting directly into the target table, bulk-insert into a staging table (no constraints, fast) and then run a single
from staging to target. This minimizes index contention on the hot target table.

def stage_and_merge(engine, records: list[dict]):
    with engine.begin() as conn:
        # 1. Fast insert into staging (no unique constraint)
        conn.execute(text("TRUNCATE TABLE events_staging"))
        conn.execute(insert(staging_table), records)

        # 2. Single merge statement from staging to target
        conn.execute(text("""
            INSERT INTO events (event_id, user_id, payload, created_at)
            SELECT event_id, user_id, payload, created_at FROM events_staging
            ON CONFLICT (event_id) DO UPDATE SET
                payload    = EXCLUDED.payload,
                updated_at = NOW()
        """))
If data is naturally partitioned by date (e.g.,
), use delete-then-reinsert per partition instead of row-level upserts. A single
is far faster than upserting 1 million individual rows.
When bulk upserting, sorting the input records by the index key before inserting reduces index page thrashing (fewer random writes):

controls how many rows are sent per individual SQL statement. Too small means many round-trips; too large means very large statements that can hit memory or network limits. 500–2,000 is a practical starting range, tune from there.
Disable indexes during bulk load, then rebuild.
For initial loads or large backfills, dropping non-unique indexes before loading and rebuilding them afterward is dramatically faster than maintaining the index during every insert.
APIs cover PostgreSQL, SQLite, and CockroachDB. For other databases, the
provide high-performance bulk write primitives with direct SQL control over
is a partition-level idempotent write for simple cases (it drops and recreates the table). For row-level upserts, combine pandas with SQLAlchemy's insert API as shown above.
provides row-level upsert. Excellent for local analytical pipelines processing Parquet files.
performs partition replacement on Hive-style tables and Delta Lake. Delta Lake additionally supports
statements based on the target warehouse.
Higher-level ORMs that wrap SQLAlchemy or motor; upsert support varies, often requiring dropping down to raw SQL for
statement is the most powerful but also the most database-specific and slowest. For analytical data warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift),
is often the only native option for row-level upserts. For transactional databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL),
SQL injection via dynamic table/column names.

The idempotency key column name and table name are sometimes constructed dynamically. Parameterization works for values but not identifiers. Always whitelist valid table and column names from a known set — never interpolate user-provided strings directly:

When multiple pipeline workers upsert to the same table concurrently, they can deadlock if they process rows in different orders and acquire row locks in conflicting sequences. Sorting records by primary key before each batch write eliminates the most common deadlock patterns.

isolation (the PostgreSQL default), two concurrent transactions can both check for a conflict, both find no existing row, and both attempt to insert — resulting in a unique constraint violation even though both used
. This is rare but possible under very high concurrency. Using
correctly handles it because the constraint violation is caught and resolved at the database level.
Idempotency keys must be globally unique across retries.

If the source system generates a new ID on retry (common in poorly designed APIs), the idempotency key will be different on each attempt — meaning every retry inserts a new row. Always verify that source IDs are stable across retries before relying on them as idempotency keys.
Monitor for unexpectedly high update rates.

upsert, every row is either an insert or an update. In a healthy pipeline, the ratio of updates to inserts should be low for new data. A spike in updates may indicate upstream reprocessing, data correction, or a pipeline bug sending stale data.
in production data pipelines — retries and replays are guaranteed to happen; the only question is whether they corrupt the data or not.
The idempotency key must come from the source system
Four strategies cover almost all cases
— row-by-row upsert loops are a performance anti-pattern that will collapse under production data volumes.
Wrap every batch in a transaction
to ensure atomicity; a failed partial write followed by a retry must behave identically to a successful first write.
by running the load function twice with identical inputs and asserting on the final row count.
Different storage systems require different techniques
